agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when and whether an order may be cancelled. The requirements involve the interplay between order lifecycle states (placed, invoiced, shipped) and cancellation eligibility.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tracked through a lifecycle of states from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order so it will not be fulfilled or shipped."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document generated for an order that locks in pricing. Finance considers invoicing irreversible."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer, representing a point-of-no-return in the fulfillment process."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial creation/confirmation of an order. Operations states this is the point at which invoicing occurs."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a product/business policy defining the cancellation window. A VP could change this policy (e.g., restrict cancellation to within 1 hour of placement).","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has not yet shipped, a cancellation request should succeed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats invoicing as an irreversible financial commitment. Cancelling an invoiced order would create accounting inconsistencies (revenue recognized then voided without a credit memo process). This is a data/state integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has been invoiced, a cancellation request must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when to invoice. A VP could change the invoicing trigger (e.g., invoice at shipment instead). However, it currently creates a hard factual constraint on the system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice must exist for that order immediately after placement."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer places an order and immediately requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — BR-1 says this should succeed (not yet shipped), but BR-2 + BR-3 together mean the order is already invoiced at placement and therefore uncancellable."},{"scenario":"Order placed but invoice generation fails or is delayed","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — If invoicing is asynchronous and hasn't completed, BR-1 would allow cancellation but this creates a race condition against BR-3's intent to always invoice at placement."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment of a multi-item order","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — unclear whether cancellation applies to unshipped line items of a partially shipped, fully invoiced order."},{"scenario":"Credit memo or refund process after invoicing","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — Finance says invoicing is irreversible, but the business presumably needs a mechanism to handle returns or errors. A credit memo process could resolve the contradiction but is not mentioned."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If the contradiction is resolved in favor of Finance (no cancellation post-invoice, i.e., never), customers lose the ability to cancel, increasing refund/return costs and potential chargebacks. If resolved in favor of Product (allow cancellation before shipment), Finance must support invoice reversal or credit memo workflows, adding accounting complexity.","operational":"Operations invoices at placement for pricing lock-in. Any resolution that decouples invoicing from placement (e.g., invoice at shipment) would require Operations to manage pricing volatility in the interim. Any resolution that adds credit memos adds workflow burden to Finance.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders that haven't shipped. If cancellation is blocked from the moment of placement, customer satisfaction and trust will suffer significantly. This is a standard e-commerce expectation.","risk":"Shipping this feature with the contradiction unresolved guarantees one of three outcomes: (1) cancellation is dead code that never succeeds, (2) invoicing integrity is violated, or (3) the system behaves inconsistently. All three are high-risk.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — their ability to cancel orders is the core feature. The contradiction may result in no cancellation being possible, which is a severe UX degradation."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Invoice integrity is at stake. If cancellation is allowed post-invoice, they need a credit memo or reversal process that does not currently exist."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Their invoicing-at-placement policy is the root cause of the conflict. They may need to adjust timing or accept a credit memo workflow."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of customer complaints if cancellation is impossible. Will need clear policy to communicate to customers."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Cannot implement without resolution. Any guess will encode the wrong business rule and require rework."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"The three requirements form a logical contradiction: BR-3 guarantees every order is invoiced at placement, BR-2 forbids cancellation of invoiced orders, and BR-1 promises cancellation before shipment. Taken together, BR-2 ∧ BR-3 → no order is ever cancellable, which directly contradicts BR-1. No acceptance criteria can be defined until stakeholders resolve this. Confidence is well below 50 due to the unresolvable contradiction.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a matter of interpretation — the three rules are formally inconsistent","Invoice and shipment are distinct lifecycle events","All three stakeholder groups have legitimate business reasons for their constraints"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept a credit memo process as a workaround","Whether Operations could decouple invoicing from placement","Whether the PM requirement assumed invoicing happens later in the lifecycle","Whether there are existing reversal or adjustment mechanisms not mentioned"],"assumptions":["Invoicing at placement means the invoice exists before any cancellation window opens","There is no existing credit memo or invoice reversal process","All three requirements are stated as hard constraints by their respective stakeholders"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are logically inconsistent. Operations invoices at placement (BR-3). Finance forbids cancellation of invoiced orders (BR-2). Therefore no order can ever be cancelled. This directly contradicts the PM requirement that orders can be cancelled before shipment (BR-1). The conjunction of BR-2 and BR-3 makes BR-1 impossible to satisfy.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support","Engineering"],"options":["A: Introduce a credit memo / invoice reversal process so that invoiced orders CAN be cancelled before shipment. Finance must accept that invoicing is reversible under controlled conditions.","B: Delay invoicing until shipment instead of placement. Operations must find another mechanism to lock in pricing (e.g., price snapshots without formal invoicing).","C: Keep invoicing at placement but allow cancellation with automatic credit memo generation — invoice remains on the books but is offset. Finance keeps irreversibility of the original invoice.","D: Accept that cancellation is not possible and remove the PM requirement. Customers cannot cancel orders."],"recommendation":"Option C is the most likely resolution. It preserves Finance's invoice integrity (the invoice is never deleted), satisfies Operations' pricing lock-in, and gives customers a cancellation path via credit memo. However, this requires Finance to confirm that a credit memo workflow is acceptable. All three stakeholder groups must convene to resolve this before any implementation begins."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot implement order cancellation as specified — the rules as stated make cancellation logically impossible.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is negated by the conjunction of BR-2 and BR-3. Any implementation would necessarily violate at least one of the three rules."}]}
